title: Cat Drooling & Foaming at the Mouth: Emergency Guide & Next Steps
meta_desc: If your cat is foaming at the mouth or drooling excessively, it could be a medical emergency. Learn common causes and why urgent vet care is vital.
content_html:
TL;DR: Sudden foaming at the mouth or excessive drooling in cats is a high-priority veterinary emergency often caused by toxins, obstructions, or severe dental pain. If your cat is also lethargic or struggling to breathe, contact an emergency veterinarian immediately.

What causes excessive drooling and foaming at the mouth in my cat?

Hypersalivation (drooling) or foaming at the mouth in cats occurs when a feline is unable to swallow saliva or is producing an overabundance of it. While minor drooling can sometimes be related to relaxation or nausea, sudden foaming is often a physiological reaction to something distressing, bitter, or toxic that the cat has come into contact with.

Is my cat's foaming at the mouth a veterinary emergency?

  • Urgency Level: High. Sudden foaming or profuse drooling should be treated as a potential emergency.
  • This behavior can indicate toxin ingestion (such as household cleaners or lilies), neurological issues, severe dental pain, or a physical obstruction in the throat.
  • Contact an emergency veterinarian immediately if your cat is also struggling to breathe, acting lethargic, or seizing.

How can taking a photo or video of my cat's drooling help the veterinarian?

  • Allows the medical team to evaluate the consistency of the foam and the cat's posture.
  • Helps the vet see any facial swelling or gum discoloration without the cat needing to exhibit the behavior again in the clinic.
  • Speeds up the diagnosis to ensure your cat receives life-saving treatment faster, even if symptoms subside by the time you reach the facility.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Drooling and foaming at the mouth in cats can be caused by a variety of factors, including oral foreign bodies, neoplasms, injuries, infectious diseases such as rabies, motion sickness, reluctance to swallow due to esophageal irritation or obstruction, sublingual lesions, tonsillitis, administration of medicine, conformational defects, metabolic disorders such as hepatic encephalopathy or uremia, and salivary gland issues. Halitosis and blood-tinged saliva are common signs, especially with caudal stomatitis or glossitis. Affected animals may paw at their mouths and resist oral examination due to pain, and regional lymph nodes may be enlarged. Before any oral examination, the possibility of rabies should be eliminated.

Chapter: Neurology, Gastroenterology, Infectious Disease, Dentistry, Emergency, Toxicology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 368)

faqs.question: Can stress cause a cat to foam at the mouth?
faqs.answer: Yes, extreme stress or motion sickness can cause drooling, but foaming is more commonly associated with toxins, bitter tastes, or underlying medical issues.

faqs.question: Should I try to wash my cat's mouth out?
faqs.answer: No, do not put anything in your cat's mouth, as this may cause them to inhale fluid into their lungs or cause them to bite you. Seek professional help immediately.

faqs.question: What are common toxins that cause foaming?
faqs.answer: Common triggers include bitter-tasting substances, topical flea medications if licked, household chemicals, and poisonous plants like lilies or sago palms.
